mnemonics.title: Ricky C. KIDS HQR
mnemonics.content: Ricky C. KIDS HQR - Remember the closure properties: Reverse, Complement, Concatenation, Kleene star, Intersection, Difference, Homomorphism, Quotient, Reverse. Actually, easier: U C I D R K H Q - Union, Complement, Intersection, Difference, Reversal, Kleene star, Homomorphism, Quotient. Wait - actually regular languages are closed under all these operations.

mnemonics.title: DAN IS CHECKING REGULARLY
mnemonics.content: DAN IS CHECKING REGULARLY - Difference, And, Not (complement), Intersection, Set difference, Concatenation, Homomorphism, Inverse Homomorphism, Quotient, Reverse, Kleene star, Union, G (generally all operations). This helps remember what operations preserve regularity.

mnemonics.title: CFLs Are Stubborn
mnemonics.content: CFLs Are Stubborn - Context-Free Languages Are Not closed under: Intersection, Complement, Set difference. This contrast helps remember what's special about regular languages.

acronyms.expansion: Deterministic Finite Automaton - used in product construction for intersection closure proof
acronyms.usage: Used to prove closure under intersection by creating product automaton

acronyms.expansion: Nondeterministic Finite Automaton - used in union, concatenation, Kleene star proofs
acronyms.usage: Simplifies closure proofs for union (parallel states), concatenation (ε-transitions), Kleene star (looping ε-transitions)

acronyms.expansion: NFA with ε-transitions - used in closure proofs for union, concatenation, Kleene star
acronyms.usage: Essential for connecting automata in closure proofs without losing regularity

visualTips.visualization: Draw two circles representing L1 and L2. Their union is the entire shaded area of both circles combined.
Draw two overlapping circles. The intersection is the common shaded area where both circles overlap.
Draw a square representing Σ*. The complement of L is everything outside the shaded region L inside the square.
Think of a grid where each cell represents a pair of states (p,q) from two DFAs. The accepting states form a diagonal pattern.
Draw the NFA with a loop arrow from accepting states back to start state with ε-label, showing how to repeat infinitely.
Replace each letter 'a' on a path with a string h(a), stretching or shrinking the path labels.

analogies.analogy: Like adding two sets of fruits - the union is all fruits from both baskets. If both baskets contain fruits (regular), the combined basket also contains fruits (regular).
Like the opposite of a light switch. If a language accepts certain strings, its complement rejects exactly those and accepts everything else - still describable by a machine.
Like finding common elements in two lists. Two DFAs 'agree' on a string only if both accept it - their 'agreement' can be tracked in a product machine.
Like translating a book from English to French. Each word (symbol) gets replaced by a phrase (string), but the structure (regularity) is preserved.
Like the word 'etc.' allowing repetition. Even though it means 'repeat forever', we can represent it with a simple machine that loops back.

analogies.analization: Like a married couple making decisions - both must agree (accept) for the pair to accept. One rejecting means the pair rejects.
